words: SAM SCHRAGER: When we stopped talking last time, ye were talking about Sam Piwash?
ARTHUR BJERKE: Yah?

speaker: SS
words: How did he make a living now, once he started doing his lumber business?

speaker: AB
words: Well, he was in the logging business as long as he lived, and he done a

speaker: AB
words: little farming up there on the Hog Meadow. And generally he had a bunch of

speaker: AB
words: them dagos a-workin* for him. Some of them was some of his relatives. And

speaker: AB
words: he had one cousin there, when Sam died, he's made a will so he got #25 a

speaker: AB
words: month as long as he lived. Oh that Sam, he was a good American even if he

speaker: AB
words: was a dago. Boy, you say anything about this country and you'd have a fight

speaker: AB
words: on your hands with old Sam.

speaker: SS
words: Did you say he got scrap lumber?

speaker: AB
words: Well, that is scrap timber, that is such a timber that, well, the only

speaker: AB
words: thing it was good for was for to make paper out of, something like that; and

speaker: AB
words: they wanted it cut out, see, to start a new forest. Well they'd give it to

speaker: AB
words: him, and he'd log it and haul it off for scrap timber, so they'd make chip

speaker: AB
words: out of it for paper. Of course it wasn't much, but they'd make a good living.

speaker: AB
words: And then he always farmed a little besides up there.

speaker: SS
words: Where did you find out that those Italians were killed when they were build

speaker: SS
words: ing the railroad?

speaker: AB
words: Well, that was generally more say about the other lumberjacks that worked

speaker: AB
words: on the railroad, see. So this is more hearsay than anything else. But they

speaker: AB
words: claim there was one buried in that fill that goes through Deary and one up

speaker: AB
words: there at Cornell. But, God knows. You know, they were all dagos, and once

speaker: AB
words: in awhile there'd be a Serbian or something else, a different breed of 'em

speaker: AB
words: that'd get in with 'em, and that one breed'd kind of have it in for him, and

speaker: AB
words: they'd get rid of him. Well, when the World's War I started, you know,

speaker: AB
words: pret'near all of them fellers disappeared—they went back to Europe. There

speaker: AB
words: was a few that stayed here, that have been here long enough and learnt to

speaker: AB
words: talk pretty good. And of course after awhile some of 'em was married and

speaker: AB
words: their kids was born here, see, and they become citizens so they stayed around.

speaker: SS
words: Did you get to know any of them, the Italians or the Bohemians that worked

speaker: SS
words: on the railroad when you were working on the railroad?

speaker: AB
words: Oh yeah, I knew several of them. And you know, they wasn't too bad fellers

speaker: AB
words: to work together with. You know, they were pret'near like the Indians—if

speaker: AB
words: they took a liking to ya, they were a damn good friend, and if they didn't

speaker: AB
words: like you, they didn't like you neither. There was one bunch that had a station

speaker: AB
words: work up here. Well, we always sold eggs and butter and stuff like that to »em,

speaker: AB
words: and once in awhile we'd butcher a beef and they'd get the beef here. Hell,

speaker: AB
words: I wouldn't want to be around nicer bunch than that crowd. They were damn nice

speaker: AB
words: fellers. And there used to be one up here in camp, his name was Sam Samowitch.

speaker: AB
words: He was kind of a good sort of a fella, and he was kind of a watchmaker too.

speaker: AB
words: He'd always sit around and monkey and patch watches for the boys in the even

speaker: AB
words: ing. He was a darn good fella. And he got so he was a section boss on all

speaker: AB
words: the railroad spurs around, so there was always a bunch of them a-workin' for

speaker: AB
words: him. And they boarded right in with the white fellers in the camp. No they

speaker: AB
words: got along pretty good with them. As I say, if they liked ya, by god they were

speaker: AB
words: good friends.

speaker: AB
words: Well it was the same way with the Indians. If you'd got to know an

speaker: AB
words: Indian, and by god if they got to liking ya, you didn't have a better friend

speaker: AB
words: than an Indian. There was some Indians that used to camp around here. Boy,

speaker: AB
words: I got along good with them. And they'd always come down and get water in

speaker: AB
words: the well here. I'd always talk to them Indians. Those days there it was so

speaker: AB
words: much huckleberries around here, and they'd come up here and pick huckleberries,

speaker: AB
words: and they'd camp all over through the woods here. Well if there was any of 'em

speaker: AB
words: camped close by, they'd generally come down and get water out of the well.

speaker: AB
words: And they'd camp just right there on the edge of the field. And there used

speaker: AB
words: to be quite a little pines along the road there, so it was pretty shady.

speaker: AB
words: Some of 'em'd camp down there.

speaker: SS
words: Would you ever go over to where they camped?

speaker: AB
words: Oh yeah, yeah, I got along pretty good with the Indian.

speaker: SS
words: What were the camps like then?

speaker: AB
words: Well they had a teepee, a teepee, y'know, and then they had the beds all

speaker: AB
words: around inside. And then it was a campfire on the outside. And by gosh, you

speaker: AB
words: know, there was years afterwards the Indians quit coming in here, you could

speaker: AB
words: see where them teepees has been, because where they had the beds, see, it'd

speaker: AB
words: shut off the air, so it'd killed the grass root. So that'd be bare, and

speaker: AB
words: there'd be a little spot in the middle, that is that the grass'd grow on;

speaker: AB
words: but there'd be a round circle that'd be bare, where they've had their beds.

speaker: SS
words: You told me about fixing a wagon tongue for that Indian. Did you have any

speaker: SS
words: other Indian friends?

speaker: AB
words: Oh yeah. Well I was pretty well friends with all of *em. Pret'near every

speaker: AB
words: one of 'em, you know, that camped up here, they'd always come down; and I

speaker: AB
words: had a pond right over there across the creek, and they'd take their horses

speaker: AB
words: over there and water »em, and got their drinking water out of the well. And

speaker: AB
words: you know them darn Indians, they could come with maybe 15, 20 horses, and

speaker: AB
words: y'know, they could herd them right in the trail right up to the pond through

speaker: AB
words: the grain, and by god they could keep them horses right in that trail! They

speaker: AB
words: didn't run all over like anybody else's horses would. No, the Indians was

speaker: AB
words: pretty good. By gosh I don't blame the Indians at all, neither, because the

speaker: AB
words: white just took the country away from them. It was really their country to

speaker: AB
words: begin with. And them Indians all come from down there on the Nez Perce

speaker: AB
words: Reservation and down there at Lapwai. Down from Kendrick, down through that

speaker: AB
words: draw there, that was pret'near all Indians in the early day. And that's

speaker: AB
words: where they was, because there wasn't much snow down in there. But then in the

speaker: AB
words: summer they'd come up in here and pick huckleberries and pick camas and catch

speaker: AB
words: a few fish.

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever see any gypsies around here?

speaker: AB
words: Oh there used to be a few of them too that'd come through, but not many.

speaker: AB
words: Oh them gypsies, goddurn, when them come along by god you better not get out

speaker: AB
words: of the house, they'd steal everything there was. And by god they'd get

speaker: AB
words: around and want a feel of ya, and by god if you wasn't careful they'd pick

speaker: AB
words: your pockets too. There was quite a few of 'em that they've picked the pockets

speaker: AB
words: on. I guess they even picked the pockets on old Nigger Doe up here. No,

speaker: AB
words: when you'd see them damn gypsies come, about the best way is just to take a

speaker: AB
words: gun and go out and meet 'em and tell them to get the hell on up the road.

speaker: AB
words: And if they didn't get, start in dropping a few balls pretty close to their

speaker: AB
words: heels. That was about the only way to take care of them. I don't think there

speaker: AB
words: is any more gypsies around now.

speaker: SS
words: Would guys come through who were just poor or beggars looking for a place

speaker: SS
words: to stay, or a handout?

speaker: AB
words: Oh yeah, there used to be quite a few of them old fellers come along and ask

speaker: AB
words: for a handout once in awhile. But a big lot of them, you know, they were

speaker: AB
words: just kind of the lumberjacks. Well they'd get a meal, and some of 'em would

speaker: AB
words: want to split a little wood for a meal, and some of 'em wouldn't. But they'd

speaker: AB
words: generally hike on. No I never had any trouble with them. They were a pretty

speaker: AB
words: good sort of a bunch of fellers, a big lot of'em. 3ust been up in the woods

speaker: AB
words: trying to get work and couldn't get work, and they'd be a-tryin' to get out

speaker: AB
words: and they were broke. Some of «em, they might have a gun or something, and

speaker: AB
words: they'd want to borrow a few dollars on that, or something like that, to get

speaker: AB
words: out. Maybe a jackknife or something, they'd try to borrow four bits or

speaker: AB
words: something on it. No as a rule they were pretty good. They didn't steal

speaker: AB
words: nothing. But they were just down and out, y'know.

speaker: AB
words: A lot of them fellas, they'd be up in here and work, well then they'd

speaker: AB
words: work and get a little money, and they'd go into Spokane. Well they'd spend

speaker: AB
words: their money and they'd come back, well maybe the camps was laid off and they

speaker: AB
words: couldn't get a job, well then they'd have to kind of bum their way out. And

speaker: AB
words: those days, you know, if you were broke and you went to Spokane, if you had

speaker: AB
words: a few pennies, say, you could go to a saloon and buy a schooner of beer, and

speaker: AB
words: pret'near all of them saloons had lunch counter. Well you buy a schooner of

speaker: AB
words: beer and then you'd go to the lunch counter and eat all what you want over

speaker: AB
words: there. That was free. By god there was lots of them old lumberjacks that'd

speaker: AB
words: put in the winter maybe for about 15, 20 cents a day—a schooner of beer, and

speaker: AB
words: go from one of them saloons to the other and get a free lunch. And along

speaker: AB
words: Christmas, y'know, they'd put up a hell of lunch around them saloons. Yes,

speaker: AB
words: and around them Scandinavian saloons, they'd put up that stockfish, a lifbefisk,

speaker: AB
words: and a lot of them Scandinavian lumberjacks, y'know, they went for that to

speaker: AB
words: beat heck.. The stockfish and then what they call the flatbrau. Well, we

speaker: AB
words: called it shinglebread—that was thin, y'know, baked on top of the stove, just

speaker: AB
words: like a cracker, but it'd be thin and crisp. Well, they'd like that and stock

speaker: AB
words: fish. Well, they'd put in their meal, the next meal maybe they'd go to another

speaker: AB
words: saloon and eat. Maybe they'd get along with two bits or so a day, so they

speaker: AB
words: didn't have to have much money 'til the camps'd start in opening, so they

speaker: AB
words: could go to camp and work again.

speaker: SS
words: I always hear that there was a lot of drinking in the camps too. What was

speaker: SS
words: that like?

speaker: AB
words: Oh they'd drink some, not too much though. Mostly on holidays, on weekends.

speaker: AB
words: During the week there wouldn't be much drinking, but there'd be quite a lot

speaker: AB
words: of poker played. There used to be one fella that was up here at Helmer and

speaker: AB
words: then there was some fellas from Bovill, they used to gamble like hell. By

speaker: AB
words: god there was one stump over across Corrall Creek, they got a-gamblin' over

speaker: AB
words: that one day, and about 12500 changed hands over that yellow pine stump over

speaker: AB
words: there one day. Well then some goddam geek, y'know, that was a smart aleck,

speaker: AB
words: he'd come down and hit them camps about weekend, and by god he'd take 'em

speaker: AB
words: all, and he'd get the hell on out. Oh yes, there was quite a lot of gambling

speaker: AB
words: around the places. And by god they gambled quite a lot right here in Deary

speaker: AB
words: too, in the early days. And then over there on the Palouse River, when they

speaker: AB
words: were a-loggin' that before the Potlatch started in, I guess that was quite

speaker: AB
words: a gambling place, what they said.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know any stories about guys gambling, or about fights over gambling,

speaker: SS
words: or any funny stories about gambling?

speaker: AB
words: No, not much, because I always stayed away from that. I didn't have no

speaker: AB
words: use for that gambling. I always stayed away from where they were a-doin' that.

speaker: AB
words: And I never did drink whiskey. I'd drink a giass of beer once in awhile.

speaker: AB
words: One time we thrashed, and I had to go to Moscow. Well we'd been thrashing

speaker: AB
words: peas and they were all mildewed, and there was quite a lot that had to be

speaker: AB
words: cleaned up around and under the feeder, and a lot of peas'd fall off. Well

speaker: AB
words: I worked around the feeder quite a lot, and of course I got quite a lot of

speaker: AB
words: that mildew. Well we got through with the last job, and it'd be nothing dry

speaker: AB
words: anymore, so we'd have to lay off a few days, and I had a little repairs to

speaker: AB
words: get for the machine. So I drove over to Moscow and got them repairs, and

speaker: AB
words: come back and got to Deary. Well we stopped and had lunch there, and the

speaker: AB
words: sack sewer was with me. "Well," I says, "what do you want to drink?"

speaker: AB
words: "Oh," he says, "let's take a beer today." Well we took a beer with our

speaker: AB
words: lunch, and boy I never got so damn sick of anything as I got from them mildew

speaker: AB
words: ed peas and that beer, and you know, I think it was 20 years before you could

speaker: AB
words: get me to touch a beer. And I don't like beer now neither. I can take a

speaker: AB
words: drink of beer, but that's plenty.

speaker: SS
words: I guess there was a lot of moonshining too, when Prohibition came in.

speaker: AB
words: Oh yeah, there was quite a lot of moonshine. Yes, there was lots of moonshining

speaker: AB
words: going on. I seen one hack go by one time that it was 16 gallon jugs

speaker: AB
words: of moonshine in it.

speaker: AB
words: (End of Side A)

speaker: SS
words: I was wondering about the trails in this country, like around here. Can

speaker: SS
words: you remember how the trails got put in in the first place?

speaker: AB
words: Well, there has been quite a few cattle in here, and sheep, and there was

speaker: AB
words: cattle trails all over in the woods. Yeah, when I cleared that field over

speaker: AB
words: there, over in the farthest end, I found a round bell in there, and that bell

speaker: AB
words: was marked in 1876. And I picked that up and brought it home.

speaker: AB
words: (My sister got to see it, and boy, she'd like to have that bell. Well,

speaker: AB
words: I says, "You can have it." And then my nephew's wife, that'd be my niece

speaker: AB
words: by marriage, she seen it, and oh she'd like to have a bell, or a bell of some

speaker: AB
words: kind. And went several years, and by gosh, I piowed one out over in the field

speaker: AB
words: there. It was full of mud, but I happened to see it was something, so I

speaker: AB
words: stopped and got off from the tractor, went back to see what it was. Begosh

speaker: AB
words: that was a bell and that was marked 1876. So I dug the dirt out of it, so

speaker: AB
words: she's got that.)

speaker: SS
words: Take this road that comes in here up Brush Creek. How did that start be

speaker: SS
words: coming a trail, and then was that the way the trail came in to the homestead?

speaker: AB
words: Yeah, but of course it was just crooked all over, back and forth. The

speaker: AB
words: first trail that come in come down over that field there, and went out the

speaker: AB
words: other way. This road you come in now from the highway, that's a road I've

speaker: AB
words: made. Oh there was just up and down hills, just an Indian trail. And that

speaker: AB
words: trail come up the meadow there, and boy how wet that'd be in the spring.

speaker: AB
words: Muddy. Oh they holler about hard times nowadays, hell they ain't seen nothing.

speaker: AB
words: By god, you could buy all the butter you wanted to for five cents a pound,

speaker: AB
words: eggs five cents a dozen, a sack of flour 49 cents, but you didn't have the

speaker: AB
words: money to buy ;it with. That was during the Cleveland time. And then the wet

speaker: AB
words: years come, well about the best a man could get in the harvest field was 75

speaker: AB
words: and a dollar a day. And then that wet year, the grain all sprouted in the

speaker: AB
words: field. Well about the only thing you could get was a little sprouted grain.

speaker: AB
words: Well here there was some of them, they'd get home, and they had a little money

speaker: AB
words: coming but couldn't get it, unless a little sprouted grain. If you had any

speaker: AB
words: hogs or any cattle, you could take that and feed them. I know Andersons up

speaker: AB
words: here, he's worked all summer, and when fall come they had 50 cents. Well they

speaker: AB
words: put that away, hid it good, maybe somebody has been in and stold it or some

speaker: AB
words: thing. They never did find that 50 cents to start the winter out. That was

speaker: AB
words: hard times too.

speaker: SS
words: How did they get through the winter?

speaker: AB
words: Well, they got through. They had a cow or two, and got ahold of a little

speaker: AB
words: flour, and a few spuds they raised. And that was just about all they had to

speaker: AB
words: eat, was a little milk and butter and bread and spuds.

speaker: SS
words: What year was this? Was this 1893, or later?

speaker: AB
words: Well about '92, along in '91 and '92.

speaker: SS
words: Did it drive a lot of people out of here, a lot of the homesteaders?

speaker: AB
words: No, by gosh, y'know, they all stayed pretty well. But then they couldn't

speaker: AB
words: file on the place, they were just squatting on the place. But there had

speaker: AB
words: been one survey over it that was surveyed during the 80's. But then it had

speaker: AB
words: to be resurveyed, and they couldn't file on the place before it was resurveyed.

speaker: AB
words: And of course it changed it quite a little. Like over where Camp 4 down there

speaker: AB
words: was, there is two section corners over there close together, and a lot of

speaker: AB
words: short 40js. And of course the same way, well now this homestead here was four

speaker: AB
words: acres. Well that is, two acres to the 40, and the same over there, and the

speaker: AB
words: same 40 down below here.

speaker: SS
words: Did a locator find this homestead for your father?

speaker: AB
words: No, he just located himself. The first place he put in a cabin was over

speaker: AB
words: across the creek here, but then they found out that was a school section, so

speaker: AB
words: he come over here and took this. And you know those days, if you didn't live

speaker: AB
words: on the place, if you moved off from the place and was off 30 days, hell some

speaker: AB
words: body else could move into your cabin. So they got the cabin built, and they

speaker: AB
words: moved up here the 17th day of March, and been here all the time since.

speaker: SS
words: Did you know of any troubles that homesteaders had around here with their

speaker: SS
words: claims getting jumped or anything like that?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, a little further east here, well some of 'em, they'd go away, and others

speaker: AB
words: had filed homestead on it. See, if they didn't get the money so they could

speaker: AB
words: file on the homestead, somebody else, that last day it was open, they could

speaker: AB
words: go in and file on your homestead. So there was quite a few that lost their

speaker: AB
words: claim that way, they didn't have money enough to file on it. And now there's

speaker: AB
words: two, three places up here, fellas didn't have money to file on their place,

speaker: AB
words: and that went back to the state land, and it still is state land.

speaker: SS
words: You mean there was no place they could borrow the money?

speaker: AB
words: Hell, there was nobody that had any money to borrow. They were all broke.

speaker: AB
words: Dad, y'know, didn't have any money to file on this place, but my sister, she'd

speaker: AB
words: been working over there at McConnell, and she had a few dollars, so she let

speaker: AB
words: dad have so he could file on the place. That's how he got the place here.

speaker: AB
words: And he filed preemption on this place, and you could live on it for three

speaker: AB
words: years, and you could prove up on it, and then you could go somewheres else

speaker: AB
words: and homestead. But dad never got the money to prove up, so dad turned it

speaker: AB
words: over to homestead instead of preemption. If he'd had them couple hundred

speaker: AB
words: dollars to file preemption, he could have homesteaded preemption on this, and

speaker: AB
words: then went and took another homestead up there on the meadows, because that

speaker: AB
words: was later that was opened. But he didn't have the money to prove up on it.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know why he picked right around here? Is it because it was open

speaker: SS
words: around here and it wasn't open other places?

speaker: AB
words: Well, it wasn't open here. But there's been bedding ground on that meadow

speaker: AB
words: there. Goddarn, y'know, got the trees cut down, and the logs skidded up into

speaker: AB
words: piles, and work it over, and seeded the grain and that grain'd grow higher

speaker: AB
words: than your head. Well, there was no other way to cut it, and used a scythe,

speaker: AB
words: but it made lots of hay. Well dad, he'd cut with the scythe, and then my

speaker: AB
words: younger sister and mother, they'd rake with the hand rakes and shock it.

speaker: AB
words: Used a kind of a long sled with a basket rack on it to haul it in. Well

speaker: AB
words: after a few years I got big enough, and dad fixed up a scythe for me. So I

speaker: AB
words: cut a lot of hay with scythe. He made a scythe handle, and he made it just

speaker: AB
words: the right length for ya. Instead of a crooked like they use them here, they

speaker: AB
words: were straight, and the end of the scythe handle'd lay on your arm, and then

speaker: AB
words: a handle hold out here, and a hand hold down here on top. I wished lots

speaker: AB
words: of time I had one of them scythe handles. But they burnt up when the old

speaker: AB
words: buildings burnt up, because they was upstairs in the old woodshed. And they

speaker: AB
words: were a whole lot easier to cut with then the scythe handles they've got now.

speaker: AB
words: Then after awhile they used to seed a little rye, and they'd cut that and

speaker: AB
words: make bundles out of it and thrash it. And Albert would fix up a kind of a

speaker: AB
words: grinder down there, so he'd grind it, and make a kind of a fine flour of it,

speaker: AB
words: so that it'd make rye bread, to eat. Well, that rye bread was pretty good.

speaker: AB
words: I wouldn't mind having a loaf of that coarse rye bread now.

speaker: SS
words: How long did your father go on using the scythe?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, 'tils he got biggest part of the stumps out of the way. There was so

speaker: AB
words: much small stumps. Well, he got ahold of a stump puller, and we pulled a

speaker: AB
words: lot of the stumps and got it so that we could get room enough to get between

speaker: AB
words: the stumps. And then the first mower he got, it was a hind-cut. It was be32

speaker: AB
words: hind, instead of afterwards, the mower was a front-cut. They were a pretty

speaker: AB
words: good mower, but they were awful heavy and were slow. They wasn't geared

speaker: AB
words: very high. You pret'near had to trot the team to get up enough motion to cut.

speaker: AB
words: Yeah, I run one of them mowers quite a lot. And then I run one.of them other

speaker: AB
words: horse mowers, a front-cut, quite a lot. They were a better mower though.

speaker: AB
words: Them hind-cuts, you know, you couldn't hardly raise the sickle on them, they

speaker: AB
words: were so damned heavy to raise the sickle. But the front-cut, you could raise

speaker: AB
words: them 18, 20 inches right off from the seat with the pedal. Well then, along

speaker: AB
words: in '28, '30, when started in getting a tractor, I got a tractor mower, and

speaker: AB
words: of course that went a whole lot faster. Well the International mower wasn't

speaker: AB
words: fast, but then I got a 3ohn Deere, that tractor mower, and hell, I could

speaker: AB
words: raise the sickle bar up four feet or better. Heck, I could cut 30, 35 acres

speaker: AB
words: a day with that.

speaker: SS
words: Did you tell me that you got a thrashing machine in those days too?

speaker: AB
words: Oh yah, yeah I got the thrashing machine in 1930. And I used that for

speaker: AB
words: quite a few years. (Well, here a week ago, after you was down the other time,

speaker: AB
words: one of the boys was down, he lives down in around Eugene, Oregon. He worked

speaker: AB
words: for me six years. He was down one evening and visited with me. He was out

speaker: AB
words: to see his siter, she lives down here at Deary.)

speaker: SS
words: I wanted to know more about some of the other homesteaders that were your

speaker: SS
words: neighbors around here, and how they made out and different things that happen

speaker: SS
words: ed to them in the early days.

speaker: AB
words: Well Martin Magnuson, y'know after I quit herding cattle for Hooker about

speaker: AB
words: 1905, well when they started in building the railroad, Magnuson, he got a

speaker: AB
words: contract to furnish the company with beef for all the railroad camps. And

speaker: AB
words: god you know, some days they'd take two, three, four beefs a day. So I got

speaker: AB
words: kind of in it, bringing in beef for him. So heck, I knew pret'near every

speaker: AB
words: farmer from Bovill to Troy, and way down below Troy, picking up beefs and

speaker: AB
words: bring 'em in. Those day it was lots of beef that was pretty wild. Oh we'd

speaker: AB
words: get some wild son-of-a-bitches. Mrs. Magnuson's brother, he was generally

speaker: AB
words: with me. Lots of times he1d start in the morning and never get in before after

speaker: AB
words: midnight with beef, if we'd strike outlaws. By god we'd have some real out

speaker: AB
words: laws sometimes. Boy, they'd fight horses and everything else. But we never

speaker: AB
words: had any trouble, unless we brought 'em in, but some of them had quite a lot

speaker: AB
words: of trouble with 'em. Well then after he quit, he started a butcher shop in

speaker: AB
words: Deary, that is after Potlatch quit beefing, he started a butcher shop in

speaker: AB
words: Deary. I still brought in quite a few cattle for him, but not very many.

speaker: AB
words: Then dad sold the timber here, he bought a 40 down on the ridge. And

speaker: AB
words: we had quite a little hay bere. And we'd bale that, baled the hay here, and

speaker: AB
words: bring our cattle down on the ridge and winter 'em down there. We had enough

speaker: AB
words: so we had enough so we could live here, and enough down there so all about we

speaker: AB
words: had to do is bring the beds and some of the eats down there, to live down

speaker: AB
words: there. And dad would bring that down in the wagon, and I'd bring the cattle

speaker: AB
words: down. The first year I'd have to have help to drive 'em down because the

speaker: AB
words: cattle wouldn't go down, but made it fine and dandy. After we'd been there

speaker: AB
words: over the winter we fed the hay down there. Well when they'd start in getting

speaker: AB
words: green grass, we'd bring 'em up here and keep 'em up here during the summer,

speaker: AB
words: because you could run 'em on the range anywheres you wanted to. And we'd live

speaker: AB
words: up here in the summer. And you know, after we'd been down there one year and

speaker: AB
words: the cattle got used to it, have to watch 'em like the dickens in fall because

speaker: AB
words: when it come that time they'd want to pull out for down on the ridge. After

speaker: AB
words: that I'd have to watch 'em to keep 'em here 'til it was ready to move. Well

speaker: AB
words: after that about the only thing I'd have to do is turn them out and start 'em,

speaker: AB
words: and boy they'd travel like...

speaker: AB
words: (End of Side B)

speaker: SS
words: Did your mother stay up here in the winter when you took the cattle down?

speaker: AB
words: No, she was down there too. No, when dad would take the bedding and that

speaker: AB
words: stuff down, she went right along, so she stayed there. But I'd come up and

speaker: AB
words: stay the night once in awhile, specially during the hunting season. I'd

speaker: AB
words: come up and hunt, maybe I'd kill a deer and bring back down.

speaker: SS
words: Was there much trouble with cattle of different people getting mixed up

speaker: SS
words: together on the range?

speaker: AB
words: No, not too much. Well, I kept a pretty good eye on our cattle here, so

speaker: AB
words: I kept them pretty well in this part down below here, because there was nobody

speaker: AB
words: else that had any cattle in there. Up that way there was a few that had cattle,

speaker: AB
words: but I'd keep our cattle out of that country and keep 'em down below.

speaker: SS
words: When you went out to get cattle for the lumber camps, did you have hard

speaker: SS
words: time tracking them down sometimes?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, not too bad. Well generally, the farmers—he'd buy the cattle, y'know,

speaker: AB
words: and they'd try have 'em in the corral, so biggest part of the time they'd be

speaker: AB
words: in the corral. Once in awhile there'd be some that'd in the canyon, we'd

speaker: AB
words: have to get out of the canyon, but then we'd have a little time.

speaker: SS
words: How would you go about doing it?

speaker: AB
words: 3ust go in and chase 'em out until we got 'em out in the open, and then rope

speaker: AB
words: 'em, and maybe tie 'em together, two and two. Then of course when they'd try

speaker: AB
words: to run out of the road, they'd hang up, so they wouldn't get away very far.

speaker: AB
words: One time we got two young bulls that we had to take out of the canyon. Boy,

speaker: AB
words: we had a time with them, but we brought 'em. And they were pretty tame when

speaker: AB
words: we got in with 'em. But I don't believe there was a whole fence between Bear

speaker: AB
words: Creek and Magnuson's place left, because them sonofaguns, they'd go right

speaker: AB
words: through the fences. Then we'd have to drive*'em out through the fence somewheres

speaker: AB
words: else again, and into the read. But we brought »em. And those days I

speaker: AB
words: didn't give a damn what kind of a horse it was neither, I could ride any horse.

speaker: AB
words: I didn't give a damn how wild or bucky it was.

speaker: SS
words: You told me about that horse you traded with the Indian for. Are there

speaker: SS
words: any other horses that you had that you can remember, that stand out?

speaker: AB
words: Well, that's the only one I traded with the Indians. I traded once in

speaker: AB
words: awhile with other people, but that'd generally be tame horses. Well, I bought

speaker: AB
words: one outlaw down on the ridge. They told me that nobody could ride that horse,

speaker: AB
words: and it always got away everytime they'd had it hooked up. Anyway I bought

speaker: AB
words: the horse. I snubbed it to my saddle horse, and I got it home, and I got it

speaker: AB
words: in the barn up there on the ridge. I fooled around with it every day. I got

speaker: AB
words: along pretty good with it. But it was hell to get it down to the water to

speaker: AB
words: water it. I'd pret'near have to take the saddle horse to lead her out there.

speaker: AB
words: One day I went down there and I put the saddle on her, and I fooled around,

speaker: AB
words: and finally I got her out. I says, "I'm going to try and ride her and see

speaker: AB
words: what happens." I got onto her and y'know, she just froze, she just stood

speaker: AB
words: there, she wouldn't move a leg. I thought to myself, "Well I can fix that all

speaker: AB
words: right." Because I always had a set of spurs in the barn, bacause they come

speaker: AB
words: awful handy when you're after wild cattle. Once in awhile you'd get one of

speaker: AB
words: them that'd laid down, and you could get off and spur them around the neck

speaker: AB
words: and they'd get up. I got off and put the spur on. I got on the saddle,

speaker: AB
words: back into the saddle, and she still didn't move. Well I raked her along the

speaker: AB
words: shoulder and the back, and boy she went into the air then! Well that was all

speaker: AB
words: right, she bucked three, four times, and boy she bucked hard! And she took

speaker: AB
words: off running. Well, I started her up towards Deary, and it was nine miles to

speaker: AB
words: Deary. I rode her into Deary, and I got off from her in Deary, and I went

speaker: AB
words: into the restaurant and got a cup of coffee and a piece of pie. I come out

speaker: AB
words: and I thought, "Maybe I'll have a little trouble when I get on her. Well I

speaker: AB
words: got on her, and by god, y'know, she went off just like an old horse. Well

speaker: AB
words: I rode her home, I didn't hurt her at all. Rode her easy so she'd cool off.

speaker: AB
words: After the time I come up here. I put her in the barn, and I gave her a kind

speaker: AB
words: of rubbing down, and one thing another.

speaker: AB
words: This was along before Thanksgiving. I kept a-takinf her out every day, and

speaker: AB
words: ride her a couple miles, and take her in and rub her down and curry her, and

speaker: AB
words: she got pretty nice and gentle. And Thanksgiving Day I thought it'd be fun

speaker: AB
words: to ride down, because I figured all of them oldtimers'd be down there at the

speaker: AB
words: Lutheran Church, it was service. I rode down there, but I waited long enough

speaker: AB
words: 'til just about the services start. I rode down there and I got off, I tied

speaker: AB
words: her up and I went in, and I sit down in the back end of the church. Quite a

speaker: AB
words: few of the boys that I knew in there, said hello to the boys that I sit down

speaker: AB
words: by. I didn't say nothing. We sit there 'til the service was out. Of course

speaker: AB
words: they all come out, and they see I had the saddle on that outlaw horse. Well,

speaker: AB
words: there wasn't a one of them that'd leave there. They was wanting to see me

speaker: AB
words: ride that horse, they thought that would be kind of fun to see. Well I

speaker: AB
words: stopped and talked to a lot of them, and finally—it was cold out there—I

speaker: AB
words: says, "I better go home." So I went down and I unloosened her , and I kinda

speaker: AB
words: scratched her a little. I stepped into the stirrup and got into the saddle,

speaker: AB
words: and I rode off just like I was a-ridin' an old horse. Well they couldn't fig

speaker: AB
words: ure out how I broke that horse in that short a time. Well I didn't have no

speaker: AB
words: trouble with her. ShB was a kind of a slender horse, and by god you know,

speaker: AB
words: I broke her to jump, and I used her after cattle quite a lot. I had her so

speaker: AB
words: that I could drive up to any of them old rail fences, and if she could put

speaker: AB
words: her head over it, she'd hop over it just like that. I could ride over any

speaker: AB
words: damn fence in the country. Well, she got so I couldn't turn her loose out

speaker: AB
words: in the pasture at all, because she'd go and jump into the field—that was

speaker: AB
words: better feeding in there. So I kept her pretty well in the barn, the time when

speaker: AB
words: I wasn't a-usin' her. But I broke her to drive, and I could drive her from

speaker: AB
words: here to Troy and back in three hours easy. That'd make about a 30 mile drive,

speaker: AB
words: and I would make that in three hours. Ten miles an hour.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of a horse was she?

speaker: AB
words: She was a kind of a roan. But I think she was kind of part hamilton

speaker: AB
words: (Hambletonian).

speaker: SS
words: What's the secret of being good with a horse, of handling a horse right?

speaker: AB
words: Well, you take a horse that's funny, I could pret'near always get along with

speaker: AB
words: an outlaw horse. And the same way, I could go to anyplace that they had a

speaker: AB
words: mad dog that'd bite; and I could go to that place and the dog wouldn't bother

speaker: AB
words: me. And the same way with yellowjackets and bees, they'd bite other people;

speaker: AB
words: but by god bees'd crawl all over me, but they wouldn't sting me. There's

speaker: AB
words: something about that. We used to cut quite a few bee trees, generally got

speaker: AB
words: the bee trees down, I'd generally have to take the honey out, because the

speaker: AB
words: other fella that'd be with me, the bees'd be after. I seen fellers try to

speaker: AB
words: come out there, and by god the bees'd drive 'em right out of the country,

speaker: AB
words: sting hell out of 'em. And they'd walk all over me and wouldn't bother me.

speaker: AB
words: It's something about it. I could get along good with 'em, other people couldn't.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think it's because you weren't afraid? Could that have been it?

speaker: AB
words: That's one thing, was a hell of a lot. You take a bee, if you are afraid

speaker: AB
words: of him and start in striking at him, they get mad. And the yellowjacket the

speaker: AB
words: same way, if you start in fighting 'em you got trouble on your hands. And

speaker: AB
words: you take if a yellowjacket come fast and light on ya like he's going to sting,

speaker: AB
words: you know what you can do that he can't sting ya? You just hold your breath

speaker: AB
words: and that yellowjacket can't sting through your hide! You can brush him off.

speaker: AB
words: No sir, he can't put a stinger through your hide if you hold your breath.

speaker: AB
words: There's a lot of 'em that don't know that. And them big bald-faced hornets

speaker: AB
words: was the same way. Them big bald-faced hornets, when they'd hit by god they'd

speaker: AB
words: hit hard! I had one of them hit me from behind that I didn't see him a-comin'.

speaker: AB
words: Boy, he damnnear knocked me over. There's been one a-monkeyin' around out

speaker: AB
words: here. But out here on the porch, sitting out there, he'd walk all over on

speaker: AB
words: my hand, arm. He wouldn't bite me, but he'd suck the moisture off from the

speaker: AB
words: pores.

speaker: SS
words: I wanted to ask you, did many of the farmers around here have cattle? Did

speaker: SS
words: they have many head of cattle back when the lumber camps were going and you

speaker: SS
words: were getting cattle for the camps?

speaker: AB
words: Not too many cows. There wasn't none of?'em that had too many cows.

speaker: AB
words: Generally two, three for home use, that was just about all they had.

speaker: SS
words: But there's a lot more pasture in this country than there is like say

speaker: SS
words: around Genesee or down around in there, isn't there?

speaker: AB
words: Oh there was quite a little more pasture, you know. There was some years

speaker: AB
words: them fellas from down below'd bring cattle up, and then generally the sheep

speaker: AB
words: men' d come in with big herds of sheep. And of course they'd raise hell with

speaker: AB
words: the pasture, because when the sheep had went over it, cattle wouldn't pasture

speaker: AB
words: where the sheep had been.

speaker: SS
words: Did many people around here have sheep?

speaker: AB
words: No, not too many. Oh we used to have a few. Where there'd be a brushy

speaker: AB
words: piece of ground we'd generally fence it in and put sheep in there. Well the

speaker: AB
words: sheep'd gnaw the bark off from the brush, and pick the Cleaves off from the

speaker: AB
words: brush, and in a couple, three years they'd kill the brush, so it'd die. So

speaker: AB
words: it was easier to clear.

speaker: SS
words: Where did these sheep herds come from that pastured up here then?

speaker: AB
words: They come from down in the Big Bend country. One outfit was the MacGregors,

speaker: AB
words: and then the Cammow (?) outfit. But they wouldn't stay but a couple, three

speaker: AB
words: days, and then they'd keep a-movin' on. Well they could move 'em along the

speaker: AB
words: county road, see. But then after years, when the cars started in going, they

speaker: AB
words: couldn't move 'em so much, they'd have to truck 'em more. Then they'd have

speaker: AB
words: to go further back in the woods.

speaker: SS
words: Did that make the guys that had cattle mad, that the sheep would come through

speaker: SS
words: and graze it out?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, well y'know there was one law: you had a piece of ground, and you could

speaker: AB
words: keep the sheep two miles away from your piece of ground. They couldn't come

speaker: AB
words: in any closer than two miles of your place, unless you let 'em. Like them

speaker: AB
words: fellers that had cattle up in here, y'know, they'd lease places. Well, they'd

speaker: AB
words: bring the cattle in, and they could keep the sheep away two miles. Well then

speaker: AB
words: they'd go over here a-ways, maybe three, four miles, and lease another place.

speaker: AB
words: Well they could keep the sheep away there. So they'd have quite a piece to

speaker: AB
words: pasture.

speaker: SS
words: You mean they wouldn't leave enough room for the sheep to get in.

speaker: AB
words: No. Well finally they made a kind of a law, there'd be one streak of

speaker: AB
words: country that the sheep could use, and the cattle could the other part. Then

speaker: AB
words: of course it didn't bother, we didn't get any more trouble with the sheep over

speaker: AB
words: on this side of the hill. They always come back on the other side of the hills,

speaker: AB
words: further towards the Palouse.

speaker: SS
words: Can you remember some more about some of the other homesteaders that were

speaker: SS
words: around in this area?

speaker: AB
words: Well Halseths, y'know, they homesteaded up here, and they were four boys.

speaker: AB
words: Old man Halseth was just sort of blind on one side, and he couldn't see much

speaker: AB
words: on the other. They homesteaded up there, and they had four boys. And the

speaker: AB
words: first kids that went up there on the nigger schoolhouse was them four boys

speaker: AB
words: and I and my sister and Hawkin Peterson and the three nigger kids. There was

speaker: AB
words: ten of us in all told, the first year. And the next year it was the two

speaker: AB
words: Thompson kids and the Blailock kid.

speaker: SS
words: How did some of these other families do? Did they have any troubles that

speaker: SS
words: you can remember?

speaker: AB
words: No, no, they got along pretty good. Like Halseths up here, he generally

speaker: AB
words: stayed home because he couldn't see very good, and Mrs. Halseth she worked

speaker: AB
words: down on the sawmill, cooked down there for the mill crew. And the oldest

speaker: AB
words: one of the boys was along, and he was kind of a flunky down there. And those

speaker: AB
words: days, y'know, pret'near everything they skidded logs with was bulls. There

speaker: AB
words: was no horses, no horse skidding, all big bulls to skid logs.

speaker: AB
words: (End of Side C)

speaker: SS
words: Can you tell me what these oxen or the cattle used to be like to use on

speaker: SS
words: the team?

speaker: AB
words: The cattle was quite a little wilder in those days than they are now.

speaker: AB
words: They were more of a wild breed. They ain't tamed down like they do now.

speaker: AB
words: Every once in awhile, god you'd find one that'd fight just like a damn tiger

speaker: AB
words: when you'd get after to chase him. Now pret'near all the cattle is pretty

speaker: AB
words: tame.

speaker: SS
words: Well what did they used to be like to pull the wagons to do the farm work?

speaker: SS
words: You said they used oxen before they had horses.

speaker: AB
words: Well you know they had a yoke over their neck, and a lot of 'em, they had

speaker: AB
words: a big staple in the yoke and just a chain by it to pull the wagon. And when

speaker: AB
words: they used the wagon, they had a slit in the yoke that they put the tongue

speaker: AB
words: into, so it couldn't run ahead. And now like plowing, they'd just hook that

speaker: AB
words: cable onto the plow, and the same thing when they were skidding logs with

speaker: AB
words: 'em, they just had a skidding tong in the end of the chain that they'd hook

speaker: AB
words: on the end of the log. Hell, a bull was a whole lot better to skid log with

speaker: AB
words: than horses. You could go in the damnedest brushiest patch with them, that

speaker: AB
words: damn big yoke, and a span of bulls, bend over trees as big as your arm.

speaker: AB
words: And you just had the damn chain and that tong to hook on the log, and then

speaker: AB
words: start 'em out with it. All you had was a switch, you didn't have a line or

speaker: AB
words: nothing. It was just "gee" and "haw," to guide them. "Gee" and "haw" was

speaker: AB
words: for right and left. And them bulls got so they'd understand that good.

speaker: AB
words: Maybe if there was a big log, they'd put on four, maybe six bulls to pull

speaker: AB
words: them out. Then they'd haul out big logs.

speaker: AB
words: No, pret'near all the sawmills around here, they used bulls. And then

speaker: AB
words: they'd just log for the mills in the winter, and they'd skid out to the skidroad

speaker: AB
words: with the bulls; and then a lot of 'em, they'd use horses and haul the

speaker: AB
words: logs from there on into the mill, haul »em on sleighs. That went pretty

speaker: AB
words: good. And the mills, they generally had big ponds. Well they'd empty them

speaker: AB
words: in the fall, and they'd drive right in the pond and unload. And god they'd

speaker: AB
words: deck them logs up higher than the ceiling in there, and then in the spring,

speaker: AB
words: when the water'd come, that'd kind of float them logs. And they'd float »em

speaker: AB
words: down, and the mill was right down at the end of the pond. Why they'd drag

speaker: AB
words: »em up a tramway and right up next to the carriage and saw 'em, see. And

speaker: AB
words: the water'd take the frost out of the logs so they'd saw pretty easy. And

speaker: AB
words: them larger sawmills generally had about 20, 25 men to saw 'em. And then

speaker: AB
words: they'd have tracks up, and a feller'd run the lumber out on dump cars, and

speaker: AB
words: then they'd pile the lumber along the tracks to let it dry. And then when

speaker: AB
words: it was dry, they'd haul it back on them dump cars and into the mill and run

speaker: AB
words: it through the planer and plane it. Hell, you know a lot of that lumber,

speaker: AB
words: eight dollars a thousand, six, eight dollars a thousand. Maybe you'd have

speaker: AB
words: to take out the logs for lumber, and then maybe haul it to Moscow, and sell

speaker: AB
words: the damned lumber for about ten dollars a thousand. So you see it was damn

speaker: AB
words: fast in making money.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know how the town of Deary got started?

speaker: AB
words: You know how it got started? Well there was a fella by the name Bill

speaker: AB
words: Deary. And there was some stores right in down below where Deary is now.

speaker: AB
words: And Bill Deary, he had a brother, he was kind of a foreman for the crew when

speaker: AB
words: they were surveying the railroad and one thing another. And I don't know,

speaker: AB
words: but this guy bought that place from Blailock, and then they decided to make

speaker: AB
words: a town out of it. And finally they got it surveyed out, town lots. Hugh

speaker: AB
words: Henry and McGowan, of course they were Potlatch men. They come up there to

speaker: AB
words: kind of run things and sell lots, and they got to selling lots. And main

speaker: AB
words: town of Deary is on the Blailock place. Well part of Deary is on three

speaker: AB
words: homesteads: it's on the Blailock place, and on the Roundtree place, and the

speaker: AB
words: Bert Crooks place. The schoolhouse and that up there is on the Bert Crooks

speaker: AB
words: place. And this (the southeast) end of Deary is on the Roundtree place.

speaker: AB
words: Bert Crooks' girls, he had two girls, they went to school the second

speaker: AB
words: year of the school down there. Their names were Delia and May. Well that

speaker: AB
words: Crooks, he was quite a booze fighter, and finally he sold the place to some

speaker: AB
words: fella; he had it, and he sold it the Potlatch Lumber Company, and Potlatch

speaker: AB
words: Lumber Company started a town there. Well Blailock he went down and he

speaker: AB
words: started a store down there by 3oel, this side of 3oeJ, and they call that,

speaker: AB
words: what in hell was...

speaker: SS
words: Cornwall? Howell?

speaker: AB
words: Cornwall, Howell was further this way. Cornwall is where Blailock started.

speaker: AB
words: (And they had one boy that growed up there, he got to be a kind of a tough

speaker: AB
words: one like the old man, drunkard, and he died, and old man died, and the old

speaker: AB
words: est girl died. I think the next to the oldest girl, I think she lives

speaker: AB
words: down there around Howell somewheres now. She got married down there, and

speaker: AB
words: I know she was there for a good many years. I can't think of their name.

speaker: AB
words: But I know she was there a few years ago, because she was up to Deary and

speaker: AB
words: I happened to meet her in there.)

speaker: SS
words: Did Deary get to be a regular town overnight, or did it take quite a

speaker: SS
words: while for it to grow up?

speaker: AB
words: Oh it growed pretty fast. Oh yeah, yes there was quite a few stores that

speaker: AB
words: got started, and got a saloon up there, and drugstore, and finally we got

speaker: AB
words: a doctor there. Oh yes, we had both a doctor and dentist, and shoemaker

speaker: AB
words: shop, oh it was quite a place at one time. And y'know when they were build

speaker: AB
words: ing roads there was lots of the lumberjacks that built shacks there, and lived

speaker: AB
words: there, that was a-workin' on the railroad. And of course when they were

speaker: AB
words: through with building the railroad, they went other places. And Helmer had

speaker: AB
words: quite a lot of fellers up there. Camp 6 was kind of a headquarter for Pot

speaker: AB
words: latch up there. They had quite a lot of people up there at Helmer that work

speaker: AB
words: ed over at Camp 6, Potlatch. Hell there was always 75 or a hundred men

speaker: AB
words: over there working up them canyons.

speaker: SS
words: What did you do after you got done working on the railroad?

speaker: AB
words: Me? Oh I mostly, mother was getting pretty old, and I mostly stayed at

speaker: AB
words: home and kinda looked after cattle—we always had ten, twelve cows—and then

speaker: AB
words: I done quite a little carpenter work around. I built quite a few houses.

speaker: AB
words: There's several houses in Deary I've built, and there's several houses in

speaker: AB
words: Helmer, some in Bovill, some down on the ridge. After mother died I got rid

speaker: AB
words: of 'em, so I only had one cow. But she was an awful good cow. I could turn

speaker: AB
words: her loose, her and the calf, and go out and watch, and she'd pret'near always

speaker: AB
words: stay right around the place. Whan I'd come home, she was pret'near close

speaker: AB
words: enough so I could call her, and she'd come in. I'd put the calf out, and

speaker: AB
words: milk maybe a quart or two. Get a little milk and turn her out. (She'd

speaker: AB
words: always work around home, and just about just time to milk her I'd call her

speaker: AB
words: in. Milk maybe a quart or two of milk, turn the calf on, and let him take

speaker: AB
words: the rest of it, then turn him in his pasture, and she'd just walk out and

speaker: AB
words: keep around close to home.) I had that cow for years. Christ she must have

speaker: AB
words: been 18, 19 years old when I sold her. And the butcher in Bovill, he'd

speaker: AB
words: always come down and wanted to know if I'd have a calf from that cow. He

speaker: AB
words: bought that calf pret'near every year. Because that calf would hogdress

speaker: AB
words: about 4, 450 pounds in the fall. Fat as a damn pig. He said that's the

speaker: AB
words: only place you could get any good beef, when he'd get that calf from me.

speaker: SS
words: Will you tell me about the work that you did with Bill Helmer?

speaker: AB
words: Well, he come in here, that was before mother died. And he was a-runnin'

speaker: AB
words: out the lines. And he come to the house and he wondered if he could stay

speaker: AB
words: here some. "Well" I says, "mother is getting pretty old, so it's kind of

speaker: AB
words: hard for her to do much cooking. But," I says, "we'll ask mother and see

speaker: AB
words: what she's got to say." So we come over to the house and talked to her.

speaker: AB
words: Mother says, "Well," she said, "all right, he can stay if he'll eat what we

speaker: AB
words: eat." Well, it was all right, he'd just stay about five days a week. So

speaker: AB
words: he come down early in the fall. And he was just alone. He said, "Say, I

speaker: AB
words: need somebody," because he was a runnin' out kind of the section lines and

speaker: AB
words: getting the section corners. He says, "Would ya want to work with me?"

speaker: AB
words: "Yah," I says, "but I can't put in a right long day."

speaker: AB
words: "Well," he says, "I got quite a little book work to do, and one thing

speaker: AB
words: another," and he says, "we wouldn't put in too long a day." Well I'd get

speaker: AB
words: up and do the chores pretty early in the morning, and turn the cows out for

speaker: AB
words: water, and feed *em in the manger so they could go out and in. We'd go out

speaker: AB
words: in the woods and we'd run lines 'tils about 2:30, 3:00 in the afternoon.

speaker: AB
words: "Well," Bill says, "we'll go in now." Well we'd go in and I could do the

speaker: AB
words: chores and milking, and he'd work on his books a little. By that time

speaker: AB
words: mother'd have supper ready. We'd always carry a sandwich with us out in the

speaker: AB
words: woods. Well we'd just mark out the old section corners. You could still see

speaker: AB
words: 'em, that'd been run in early days. So we'd work them out, and marked the

speaker: AB
words: corner trees up high, so we could see 'em before the snow got too deep.

speaker: AB
words: And then when the snow got big, then he started running the railroad track,

speaker: AB
words: because the snow got high, so we didn't have to bend down the biggest part

speaker: AB
words: of the brush, so we could look over the biggest part of the brush. Well

speaker: AB
words: then cut stakes, and put down a stake about every 50 feet where you wanted

speaker: AB
words: the railroad tracks, and run out the crooks and curves. So we worked on

speaker: AB
words: that all winter. So I done pretty good that winter.

speaker: SS
words: What was Bill Helmer like?

speaker: AB
words: He was pretty dark, skinny, he wasn't a too heavy a man. By god that

speaker: AB
words: fella could travel in the woods too. They all told me "You'll never be able

speaker: AB
words: to keep up with Bill Helmer," but Iwas pretty good myself. After the snow

speaker: AB
words: got deep we used snowshoes, and of course we'd have our trail broke, and

speaker: AB
words: coming to or going out in the morning, we generally had the snowshoe trails

speaker: AB
words: to work on. So we'd get along pretty good. No, I got along pretty good

speaker: AB
words: with Bill Helmer. And that spring when he had the surveying done right in

speaker: AB
words: the neighborhood all around here, so he they had to move, he said to me,

speaker: AB
words: "I uishj' he says, "you coulda went with me. Igot along with you just about

speaker: AB
words: as good as anybody." Because Inever had lost(?) anything, and Iwas pretty

speaker: AB
words: handy with the compass myself. When he'd get tired of using it, by god I'd

speaker: AB
words: use it. So we kinda changed off.

speaker: SS
words: You say that most of the work you did was a resurvey?

speaker: AB
words: Yeah we had to go over them see, because there was pieces of timber see,

speaker: AB
words: that they bought from the farmers and others from state land. So they had

speaker: AB
words: to keep track of that. So they had to keep the state land and what they had

speaker: AB
words: bought from farmers separate. And some of it they hadn't even bought. So

speaker: AB
words: it had to be kept apart.

speaker: SS
words: What were the woods like where you were surveying them? What was the

speaker: SS
words: timber like there before it was cut?

speaker: AB
words: Oh it was pretty open timber, because the Indians used to burn it, but

speaker: AB
words: it started in growing up again before the Potlatch got a-loggin'. So it

speaker: AB
words: got quite alot of brush. But when Ifirst come in, remember, you could

speaker: AB
words: drive pret'near anywheres with a team and wagon.

speaker: SS
words: Where were you working from here, the two of you?

speaker: AB
words: When we was...

speaker: SS
words: Surveying.

speaker: AB
words: Well we worked all over on this township, and clean up there on the Cherry

speaker: AB
words: Butte and all over clean into Deary. We pret'near laid out all these rail

speaker: AB
words: road tracks here that winter. Clean over on Corral Creek and over around

speaker: AB
words: where Camp 4 is. On this side of Camp 4, there is a bunch of short forties

speaker: AB
words: in there. They're wide enough one way but they're too short the other way.

speaker: AB
words: And at Camp 4 there's two sections...

speaker: AB
words: (End of Side D)

speaker: SS
words: Would you say that again?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, I said it was terrible that they kind of figured that maybe killed

speaker: AB
words: fellers in here that had places. Of course they sold out and moved out, but

speaker: AB
words: they never could prove nothing on them. The Shea Meadow, they always figured

speaker: AB
words: there was a man killed up there, but nobody could prove nothing. So there

speaker: AB
words: you are. No, I say I have an idea there was several men killed up in this

speaker: AB
words: country in the early days that nobody knew anything about. If somebody come

speaker: AB
words: along they thought they had any money, somebody hit him in the head and buried

speaker: AB
words: him somewheres and be done with it.

speaker: SS
words: And you would hear rumors about it when you were young?

speaker: AB
words: Oh there was lots of rumors going around about several people, but nobody

speaker: AB
words: could say anything. About the only thing that everybody could do was to

speaker: AB
words: keep their mouth shut, to keep out of trouble.

speaker: SS
words: You mean there were suspicions that people that lived around here had done

speaker: SS
words: it?

speaker: AB
words: Oh yes, quite a lot of them, back up in through there, homesteaders that

speaker: AB
words: was suspicioned of killing some fella that'd-come through that prospected,

speaker: AB
words: that might have a little money. Well they'd disappear, and nobody»d ever see

speaker: AB
words: anything anymore of 'em. Well what become of 'em?

speaker: SS
words: Would homesteaders disappear too?

speaker: AB
words: No...You know there was a few of them homesteaders that was kind of suspicioned

speaker: AB
words: of doing that.

speaker: SS
words: Would people still be as friendly as ever to them?

speaker: AB
words: Well, they'd talk when they'd meet 'em. Well, they couldn't prove nothing.

speaker: AB
words: They didn't know more whether they'd killed a man than I knew whether you

speaker: AB
words: have killed one. So they couldn't say nothing. And about the only thing

speaker: AB
words: that you could do'd be friendly when you'd meet 'em. But they might kind

speaker: AB
words: of have a suspicion of it. Like them fellas that had the Shea Meadow. There

speaker: AB
words: was a fella that come along and stayed with them. He'd been prospecting,

speaker: AB
words: and he seemed to have a little money right along. Well, finally he disappear

speaker: AB
words: ed. Well they said he left the country. Well that's all they know. The

speaker: AB
words: Sheas still stayed up there. But awhile after he died, they tore down a

speaker: AB
words: big log fence they had there, and they had a big fire in there, and they

speaker: AB
words: figured that they had him buried in there and burnt over it. A lot of them

speaker: AB
words: kind of thought the Sheas has killed him, but they couldn't say nothing be

speaker: AB
words: cause nobody knew anything.

speaker: SS
words: Were there any people around that were known to be crooks or thieves, that

speaker: SS
words: let's say would steal from another homesteader?

speaker: AB
words: Oh. There was one outfit that I don't think was too damn straight. A

speaker: AB
words: fella got crazy and he went and hung himself, and of course he had folks

speaker: AB
words: in the old country and around. I know they sold everything out of his house

speaker: AB
words: and other places too that picked up things. But Iain't going to say who they

speaker: AB
words: were because there's some of the same outfit right in the country today, but

speaker: AB
words: they seem to get along pretty good now. And they were kind of a bunch that'd

speaker: AB
words: fight in among themselves. They had some awful fights in among themselves.

speaker: AB
words: But they got along pretty good with other people. But there was two

speaker: AB
words: brothers there, by damn they'd get a few drinks, one would try to kill the

speaker: AB
words: other one, all the time.
